Nice to meet you, Flavia. I am a buyer who works for an Italian food company.
Nice to meet you, Marco. I am a consultant who helps small Brazilian producers export.
Great. Can you explain what a geographic indication is, exactly?
Sure. It is a certification that shows the origin of a product from one special territory.
I see. My company only buys artisanal coffee that has a clear denomination.
Perfect. Our producers make artisanal coffee, and the label that you see proves its origin.
It is a designation that connects a product to a territory.
The label that you see proves its denomination.

Good morning. Let me explain my role. I am a consultant who works with small businesses in Brazil. My job is to explain our products to international partners. Today, I want to talk about geographic indications. A geographic indication is a certification that shows the origin of a product. It is a designation that connects a product to a territory. For example, our coffee is artisanal, and it comes from one special region. The label that you see on the box proves its denomination. The producers who make it follow strict rules. So, a geographic indication is a promise that protects both the origin and the quality. Thank you.

Hello. Here is one simple tip to explain your role in a meeting. Do not use long, difficult words. Use short sentences that define each idea. When you explain a thing, use that or which. A certification is a document that proves the origin. When you explain a person, use who. A producer is a person who makes the product. This is the secret: one clear idea, one relative clause. When you explain your role like this, everybody understands you. Good luck!

First, I explain my role and the institution that I represent. Then, I describe the certification that protects the product. Next, I explain the origin and the territory where it is made. After that, I talk about the producers who make it. Finally, I show the label that proves its denomination.
